feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced, nitrogen-forward (e.g. 10-10-10 to 16-16-8) for leafy top growth

commonIssues: Onion maggot bores into stems, causing plants to turn yellow and wilt (UMN Extension).
Fungal/bacterial rots: Fusarium basal rot, Botrytis neck rot, and bacterial soft rot, encouraged by overwatering and waterlogged soil (UMN Extension).
Shallow roots dry out fast; keep evenly moist and never waterlogged. Use a pot at least 6-8 inches (15-20 cm) deep with good drainage.
Weeds easily outcompete small seedlings early in the season (UMN Extension).
Perennial in USDA zones 6-9; north of zone 6 typically grown as an annual (Missouri Botanical Garden).

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing scallions in home gardens. Full sun required; keep soil evenly moist (shallow roots); soak thoroughly at least once weekly if under 1 inch rain; sow 1/4 to 1/2 inch deep, rows 12-18 inches apart, thin to 1 inch; succession-sow every 3-4 weeks.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing scallions in home gardens. Onions require full sun for best growth.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing scallions in home gardens. Pests/diseases: onion maggot; Fusarium basal rot, Botrytis neck rot, bacterial soft rot; weed competition early season.

Oregon State University, Commercial Vegetable Production — Onions, Green (Bunching). Germination 46-86 F (8-30 C), optimum over 60 F (16 C); maintain top foot of soil at 65-70% of field capacity; nitrogen 50-70 lb/acre with side-dressing 75-100 lb N/acre; seeding March through mid-August; 60-70 day crop.
Oregon State University, Commercial Vegetable Production — Onions, Green (Bunching). Nitrogen-driven leafy crop; side-dress additional nitrogen during growth; seeding window March through mid-August.
Oregon State University, Commercial Vegetable Production — Onions, Green (Bunching). Seeding March through mid-August; 60-70 day crop used for irrigation planning.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Allium fistulosum. Winter hardy to USDA Zones 6-9; full sun to part shade; medium water; clumping evergreen perennial, grown as annual north of Zone 6.

Iowa State University Extension, Yard and Garden — The Other Onions. Allium fistulosum is hardy and one of the first plants to emerge in spring; grown primarily for green tops; sow early spring and again midsummer for fall/following-spring harvest.

Container size estimated from extension guidance that scallions have shallow roots needing a pot 6-8 inches (15-20 cm) deep with good drainage (UMN Extension watering note plus general extension container guidance); exact liter volume is not given by a single .edu page, so recommendedMinLiters (7 L) is a conservative practical estimate for a 6-inch-deep, ~25 cm-wide pot.

coldHardyMinC (-12 C) is a conservative value: Missouri Botanical Garden lists USDA Zones 6-9 (Zone 6 minimum approx -23 to -18 C), but reliable garden overwintering of green tops is more conservative; RHS hardiness rating H5 (-15 to -10 C) for Welsh onion supports roughly -12 C as a safe operating floor for keeping plants productive rather than absolute survival.

feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays (21 days) estimated: Oregon State specifies total seasonal nitrogen and side-dressing for field/acre but no home-garden day interval; 3-week feeding cadence is a conservative translation for a fast leafy nitrogen-hungry crop.
